_README: PLACEHOLDER question bank. These are generic maritime-style practice questions written to exercise the chatbot flow. They are NOT official AMSA exam questions and must be replaced/approved by an AMC Search subject-matter expert before real use. Keep the same field structure when editing. modelAnswer and keywords never leave the server (see server.js), so students cannot read the answer key.

questions.questionText: You are the give-way vessel in a crossing situation with a power-driven vessel on your starboard side. What action should you take?
questions.modelAnswer: Under COLREGs Rule 15, the vessel which has the other on her own starboard side is the give-way vessel and must keep out of the way. Take early and substantial action, and if the circumstances admit, avoid crossing ahead of the other vessel.

questions.questionText: What lights would you expect to see on a power-driven vessel underway at night, viewed from directly ahead?
questions.modelAnswer: From directly ahead you would see the forward masthead light (white), and both sidelights - red on the port side and green on the starboard side. A vessel over 50m also shows a second, higher after masthead light.

questions.questionText: Describe the immediate actions you would take on hearing a 'man overboard' alarm.
questions.modelAnswer: Release the lifebuoy with light and smoke on the side the person fell, post a dedicated lookout pointing at the person, press the MOB button/note the position, sound the alarm, and commence a recovery manoeuvre such as a Williamson turn while informing the master.

questions.questionText: What is a muster list and what information must it contain?
questions.modelAnswer: A muster list is a document required under SOLAS that sets out the emergency signals, the actions the crew must take when those signals are sounded, the assigned emergency stations and duties for each crew member, and details of substitutes for key persons who may become disabled.

questions.questionText: What are the three components of the fire triangle, and how does removing each one extinguish a fire?
questions.modelAnswer: The fire triangle is heat, fuel and oxygen. Removing heat is cooling (e.g. water), removing fuel is starving, and removing oxygen is smothering. Taking away any one side of the triangle extinguishes the fire.

questions.questionText: Explain what is meant by the term 'GM' and why a positive GM is important.
questions.modelAnswer: GM is the metacentric height, the vertical distance between the centre of gravity (G) and the metacentre (M). A positive GM means G is below M, giving the vessel a righting moment that returns her upright when heeled - it is a measure of initial stability. A negative GM risks loll or capsize.

questions.questionText: You are approaching a lateral buoy showing a single red can shape in IALA Region A. What does it indicate and how should you pass it?
questions.modelAnswer: In IALA Region A a red can-shaped buoy is a port-hand lateral mark. When entering a channel or proceeding in the direction of buoyage, you keep it on your port side (leave it to port).

questions.questionText: What checks would you carry out before starting a main diesel engine from cold?
questions.modelAnswer: Confirm sufficient lube oil and cooling water levels, fuel available and valves lined up, adequate starting air pressure, turning gear disengaged, and turn the engine on the turning gear with indicator cocks open to check for water or oil in the cylinders before starting.

questions.questionText: Describe the purpose of a lubricating oil system on a marine diesel engine.
questions.modelAnswer: The lube oil system reduces friction and wear between moving parts, carries away heat (cooling), helps clean the engine by carrying contaminants to filters, and assists sealing between piston rings and liner. It circulates filtered oil to bearings and running surfaces.

questions.questionText: What is the function of a bilge system on board a vessel?
questions.modelAnswer: The bilge system collects and removes accumulated water and drainage from bilge wells throughout the vessel and pumps it overboard or to a holding tank, passing oily water through an oily water separator, helping keep spaces dry and preventing progressive flooding.

questions.questionText: Why is a sea water cooling system used on marine engines, and what is a common risk with it?
questions.modelAnswer: Sea water provides an abundant heat sink to cool the engine, usually via a heat exchanger cooling a closed fresh water circuit. Common risks are corrosion, marine growth fouling and blocked strainers, which reduce cooling flow - so strainers are checked and cleaned regularly.

questions.questionText: What VHF channel is designated as the international distress, safety and calling channel, and what word precedes a distress call?
questions.modelAnswer: VHF Channel 16 is the international distress, safety and calling channel. A distress call is preceded by the word 'Mayday', spoken three times.

questions.questionText: In restricted visibility, you hear a fog signal apparently forward of your beam. What does COLREGs require of you?
questions.modelAnswer: Under COLREGs Rule 19, unless you have determined a risk of collision does not exist, you must reduce speed to the minimum at which you can be kept on course, and if necessary take all way off, and navigate with extreme caution until the danger of collision is over.

questions.questionText: What personal protective equipment would you expect a crew member to wear when working on deck in heavy weather?
questions.modelAnswer: In heavy weather on deck a crew member should wear a lifejacket and a safety harness clipped to a lifeline, non-slip footwear, high-visibility clothing, gloves and a helmet as appropriate to the task.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between a magnetic compass and a gyro compass, and why carry both?
questions.modelAnswer: A magnetic compass points to magnetic north and needs no power but is subject to variation and deviation. A gyro compass uses a spinning rotor to seek true north and is not affected by magnetism, but depends on electrical power. Both are carried so the magnetic compass provides a backup if the gyro or power fails.
